did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach for melanoma recognition in dermoscopy images. Specifically, it proposes a network based on DenseNet (without fully connected layers) combined with an attention module and a novel feature confusion regularization technique. The model was trained on the ISIC 2017 dataset and evaluated on ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2019 datasets to classify melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' and presents a novel deep learning approach (DenseNet with attention and feature confusion regularization) for melanoma recognition. It includes original experimental methods, training on the ISIC 2017 dataset, evaluation on ISIC 2016 and 2019 datasets, and reports new performance metrics (e.g., average precision 0.694, sensitivity 0.693), which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Multi‑level contexts aggregation for melanoma recognition under feature confusion regularization
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma recognition in dermoscopy images by proposing a novel deep learning network that combines DenseNet without fully connected layers, an attention module, and a feature confusion regularization technique. The model is designed to capture multi-level local contexts and global contexts while preventing overfitting on small, imbalanced datasets by reducing the L2 distance between features of different labels. The network was trained on the ISIC 2017 dataset without using extra data and evaluated for robustness on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2019 datasets. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method outperformed state-of-the-art studies, achieving an average precision of 0.694 and sensitivity of 0.693 on the ISIC 2017 test set. Furthermore, evaluation on ISIC 2019 showed improvements of 8% in average precision and 3% in average sensitivity, confirming the model's robustness and suitability for clinical application.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for recognition in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) on ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2016. Also one of 8 classes in ISIC 2019 evaluation (4522 images in training set).
Composite class used as the negative class in the binary classification task on ISIC 2017 (1626 images) and ISIC 2016. In ISIC 2019, this category is disaggregated into specific classes.

One of 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset evaluation (12,875 images in training set). Used in feature confusion regularization where features from NV were paired with features from the rest of the seven categories.
One of 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset evaluation (3323 images in training set).
One of 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset evaluation (867 images in training set).
One of 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset evaluation (2624 images in training set).
One of 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset evaluation (239 images in training set).
One of 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset evaluation (253 images in training set).
One of 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset evaluation (628 images in training set).

all_task_types.target: Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma (binary) and 8-class skin lesion classification
all_task_types.details: The paper performs melanoma recognition, which is a binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) on the ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2016 datasets. It also evaluates the model on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which involves classifying images into 8 specific skin lesion categories (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesion, Squamous cell carcinoma). The model outputs prediction probabilities for these discrete classes using a SoftMax normalization and a classification layer.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset used for melanoma recognition. It contains images of melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions.

use.training: The proposed network was trained on the ISIC 2017 training set without using extra data. The validation set was used to decide whether to save the current optimal model parameters based on AUC value during training.
use.validation: Used for model selection during training on ISIC 2017.
use.testing: The proposed network was tested on the ISIC 2017 test set to evaluate performance (AP, SEN, AUC, etc.).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset used to test the robustness of the network trained on ISIC 2017.

use.training: A subset of 750 images from the ISIC 2016 training set was used for fine-tuning the model in one robustness experiment.
use.validation: A subset of 150 images from the ISIC 2016 training set was used for validation during the fine-tuning process.
use.testing: The model was tested on the ISIC 2016 test set (379 images) to evaluate robustness. One experiment involved direct testing without fine-tuning, and another involved fine-tuning on the ISIC 2016 training split.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset containing 8 classes of skin lesions, used to evaluate the network's performance on multi-class classification and robustness.

use.training: The original ISIC 2019 dataset was split into training (80%), validation (10%), and testing (10%) sets. The network was trained on the 80% split for 100 epochs.
use.validation: The 10% validation split was used to decide whether to save the current optimal model parameters based on Average Precision (AP) value during training.
use.testing: The model was evaluated on the 10% testing split to assess multi-class classification performance and robustness.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To reduce the L2 distance between features of samples with different labels to prevent overfitting on small training sets.
To reweight loss contributions and focus on difficult samples, particularly for handling label imbalance.
To average predictions on 64 augmented copies of each test image to form the final prediction, improving robustness.
To decide whether to save optimal model parameters during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (AUC, AP, ACC, SEN, SPE) and percentage improvements without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2017.notes: The proposed network outperformed state-of-the-art studies, especially for average precision (0.694) and sensitivity (0.693), which are improved by 2.2% and 3.5% respectively compared to previous best methods without extra data. The AUC (0.880) is extremely close to the best value (0.883 by Yan et al.).

Proposed Network (Fine-tuned).notes: Fine-tuned on ISIC 2016 training set (900 images) and tested on ISIC 2016 test set (379 images). Gained best performance among compared methods.

Proposed Network (No Fine-tuning).notes: Tested directly on ISIC 2016 test set without fine-tuning. Confirmed robustness.

First place method [1] in ISIC 2016 chal.notes: Compared against; proposed network confirmed robustness.

ISIC 2016.notes: Evaluation on ISIC 2016 confirmed the network is robust enough on different dermoscopy image datasets. The network gained excellent robustness because it is trained end-to-end without using complex preprocessing such as hair removal.

Proposed Network.notes: Improved by 8% in average precision and 3% in average sensitivity compared to previous state-of-the-art. Evaluated on 8 classes: Melanoma (MEL), Melanocytic nevus (NV), Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), Actinic keratosis (AK), Benign keratosis (BKL), Dermatofibroma (DF), Vascular lesion (VASC), Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC).

ISIC 2019.notes: Evaluation on ISIC 2019 indicates the network improves by 8% and 3% for average precision and average sensitivity respectively, showing it is robust and suitable for clinical application. The model was trained on the 80% split for 100 epochs.

architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (cropped from images resized to max 1024 pixels)

architecture_details.backbone: DenseNet161 without FC layers to keep multi-scale skin lesion features flowing between layers
architecture_details.blocks: Four Dense Blocks: LastDB (captures global context), InterDB-2 and InterDB-3 (capture local contexts in different receptive fields)
architecture_details.transition_layers: Transition Layers between adjacent Dense Blocks reduce feature dimension by a factor of 0.5 (channel-wise and spatial-wise)
architecture_details.attention_module: Designed to focus on local contexts in InterDBs guided by global context from LastDB; uses global average pooling
architecture_details.aggregation_module: 1x1 convolutions reduce channel-wise dimension; parameter-free global average pooling on local and global feature maps; outputs concatenated as entire vector

training_details.epochs: 100 epochs (for ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2019); 50 epochs (for fine-tuning on ISIC 2016)
training_details.learning_rate: 0.001 (initialized for ISIC 2017/2016); 0.002 (initialized for ISIC 2019); decayed by 0.1 after 10 and 30 epochs
training_details.data_augmentation: Offline resizing; online random rescaling (0.8-1) and 224x224 center crop; test-time augmentation with 64 augmented copies averaged
training_details.model_selection: Save optimal parameters based on AUC (for ISIC 2017/2016) or Average Precision (for ISIC 2019) on validation set

classification_or_output_head.type: SoftMax normalization followed by classification layer
classification_or_output_head.loss_function: Combined Focal Loss and Feature Confusion Regularization

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: First place method [1] in ISIC 2016 challenge
preprocessing_techniques: Offline resizing of images to a width or height of no more than 1024 pixels
Offline repetition of melanoma images in the training dataset to balance class distribution
Online random rescaling of input images (0.8–1)
Online center cropping of 224 × 224 pixel areas
Test-time augmentation (TTA) with 64 augmented copies of each test image averaged for final prediction
SoftMax normalization of features to stabilize training

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Repeated melanoma images in the training dataset offline to balance class distribution.
Randomly rescaled input images with a range of 0.8–1 during training.
Cropped a 224 × 224 pixel area at the center of the rescaled image during training.
Augmented each test image 64 times using the online augmentation method (rescaling and center cropping) and averaged the predictions on the 64 augmented copies to form the final prediction.

conclusions: The proposed network, which combines DenseNet without fully connected layers, an attention module, and feature confusion regularization, outperforms state-of-the-art methods for melanoma recognition.

The method achieved an average precision of 0.694 and sensitivity of 0.693 on the ISIC 2017 test set, representing improvements of 2.2% and 3.5% respectively over previous best methods without using extra data.

The feature confusion regularization effectively prevents overfitting on small training sets by reducing the L2 distance between features of samples with different labels, thereby improving feature representation ability.

The attention module successfully focuses on multi-level local contexts of skin lesions guided by global context, automatically reducing the impact of irrelevant artifacts without requiring complex preprocessing like hair removal.
Aggregating local contexts from intermediate Dense Blocks with different receptive fields alongside global context from the last Dense Block significantly improves melanoma recognition performance.
The model demonstrates robustness when tested on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2019 datasets, showing an 8% improvement in average precision and a 3% improvement in average sensitivity on ISIC 2019.

The proposed network is suitable for clinical application due to its robustness across different dermoscopy image datasets and its ability to handle challenges such as small training datasets, label imbalance, and intra-class variation.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC 2017 dataset, which exhibits significant label imbalance (374 melanoma vs. 1626 non-melanoma images), potentially biasing the model towards the majority class despite mitigation efforts.

The proposed feature confusion regularization and attention module were designed to handle small training sets and noisy backgrounds, but the effectiveness may be limited to the specific distribution of the ISIC datasets.

Robustness was evaluated on ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2019, but the ISIC 2019 evaluation involved splitting the training set (80/10/10) rather than using a held-out public test set with ground truth, which may overestimate generalization performance.

The model achieved state-of-the-art performance on ISIC 2017 without using extra data, outperforming methods that utilized additional datasets or segmentation masks, highlighting its efficiency but also its dependence on the specific ISIC data distribution.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline methods.

The claim of suitability for clinical application is based on robustness metrics across datasets rather than prospective clinical trials or external validation on independent, real-world clinical cohorts.
